evidence_quotes.quote: Video games are one of the most important and widely consumed cultural products of our time, yet more and more of them are being completely removed from existence, making it completely impossible to experience them, creating a cultural vacuum such that future generations will have a huge gap where the culture of our time would exist.

When the company decides that the cost of maintaining servers exceeds what they expect to gain from selling more copies of the game, shut down the servers permanently, leaving the games completely unusable.

paragraphs.text: Dear Sirs: We are at a historic moment for the erosion and loss of consumers when it comes to digital rights. I would like to draw attention to a specific problem that is seriously eroding Europe’s cultural paradigm, a problem that is currently not the target of this law but which I believe is extremely related and should be attacked. Video games are one of the most important and widely consumed cultural products of our time, yet more and more of them are being completely removed from existence, making it completely impossible to experience them, creating a cultural vacuum such that future generations will have a huge gap where the culture of our time would exist. This is happening for the following: more and more companies are setting up their games as ‘Live service’. This is both a business model and a technical model where the end-user has only a small part of the software needed to run the game, the rest being exclusively on the servers of the developer company. When the company decides that the cost of maintaining servers exceeds what they expect to gain from selling more copies of the game, shut down the servers permanently, leaving the games completely unusable. Apart from the moral and cultural aspect, this is an incredible violation of consumer rights. Players are buying a product, and the developer is arbitrarily and unilaterally deciding when the product ceases to function permanently. A very good comparison would be a publisher who sells a novel but, when he sees sales decline, not only stops printing new editions, but remotely activates a chip that makes all existing copies of the novel fire. In short, a company is destroying the property for which its users have paid. My petition is simple: make this anti-consumer practice impossible. Oblige developer companies to have a plan for the completion of the support, such that when they switch off their servers, all users have the ability to continue using the product for which they paid. This can be achieved in several ways: the company can release the software and documentation on its servers in such a way that technical users can recreate it. They can make a patch before the end of the support that allows the game to be played offline without servers. Or they can make all the software necessary to run the game live on the customer received by the users, rather than being distributed between customer and server. This is a very serious problem not only for today’s users, but also for tomorrow’s users. Today they are video games, but we are already starting to see similar cases in other areas, such as Amazon and Adobe, which are starting to remotely sabotage their old products to become unusable when they are no longer profitable, forcing them to buy the new ones. I hope you will seriously consider this huge problem in order to solve it in this draft law, defending and empowering consumers across the EU against these predatory and abusive practices. Many thanks!
